source_paper_title: Asymptotic Optimality of Constant-Order Policies in Joint Pricing and Inventory Models
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Chen_2024, title={Asymptotic Optimality of Constant-Order Policies in Joint Pricing and Inventory Models}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1367}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1367}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Chen, Xin and Stolyar, Alexander L. and Xin, Linwei}, year={2024}, month=feb, pages={557–577} }
context:
Page 6, Section 3.1 “Additional definitions and notations”, Remarks (bullet discussing moment assumptions).

By combining the arguments of the present paper (which assumes finite third moment but allows \(D\) to have bounded support) with the arguments of the aforementioned earlier version, we would in principle obtain asymptotic optimality assuming only a finite second moment. For simplicity of exposition, we do not formalize this generalization, and leave as an open question the minimal required assumptions on \(D\) for such an asymptotic optimality to hold.
title: Determine minimal moment assumptions for asymptotic optimality of constant-order policies
background_latex:
Consider the single-item, periodic-review, lost-sales inventory model with deterministic lead time \(L\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}\) and planning horizon \(T\in\mathbb{Z}_{>L}\). Demands \(\{D_t\}_{t\ge 1}\) are i.i.d. copies of a nonnegative, non-deterministic random variable \(D\) with finite mean \(\mu=\mathbb{E}[D]\) and variance \(\sigma^2=\mathrm{Var}(D)\in(0,\infty)\) (and possibly higher moments). Inventory on hand at the start of period \(t\) is \(I_t\ge 0\), and the pipeline vector is \(x_t=(x_{1,t},\dots,x_{L,t})\in\mathbb{R}_+^L\), where \(x_{i,t}\) is the quantity scheduled to arrive in \(i\) periods. Each period: (1) \(x_{1,t}\) arrives and is added to inventory; (2) the controller places an order (a nonnegative quantity measurable with respect to current state and model primitives) that becomes \(x_{L,t+1}\); (3) demand \(D_t\) realizes; (4) end-of-period inventory is \(I_{t+1}=(I_t+x_{1,t}-D_t)^+\) and lost sales are \(N_t=(I_t+x_{1,t}-D_t)^-\). Costs per period are holding \(h I_{t+1}\) and lost-sales penalty \(c N_t\) with \(h,c>0\), so \(C_t=h(I_t+x_{1,t}-D_t)^+ + c(I_t+x_{1,t}-D_t)^-\). The admissible policies \(\Pi\) are all nonanticipative ordering rules using \((t,T,L,h,c,\text{law of }D,I_t,x_t)\). The performance criterion is expected total cost \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t]\) with fixed initial state \(I_0=0\) and \(x_0=0\). Let \(\mathrm{OPT}(L,T)=\inf_{\pi\in\Pi}\mathbb{E}[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t^{\pi}]\).

A (stationarized) constant-order policy is parameterized by \(r\in[0,\mu)\): it orders \(I_\infty^r+r\) in the first period and then orders \(r\) each subsequent period, where \(I_\infty^r\) has the steady-state waiting-time distribution of a stable \(GI/D/1\) queue with interarrival distribution \(D\) and deterministic service time \(r\), i.e. \(I_\infty^r\stackrel{d}{=}\sup_{k\ge 0}(kr-\sum_{i=1}^k D_i)\). Let \(z\in\arg\min_{v\ge 0}\big(h\,\mathbb{E}[I_\infty^v]-cv\big)\) (take the smallest minimizer if not unique), and denote by \(\pi_z\) the corresponding constant-order policy. The paper proves asymptotic optimality of \(\pi_z\) as \(L\to\infty\) under the standing assumption that \(\mathbb{E}[D^3]<\infty\) (finite third moment).
problem_definition_latex:
Find the weakest (minimal) integrability/moment assumptions on the demand distribution \(D\) (beyond nonnegativity, non-determinism, and \(\mu=\mathbb{E}[D]<\infty\)) under which the constant-order policy \(\pi_z\) is asymptotically optimal as lead time grows, in the sense that
\[
\lim_{L\to\infty}\;\limsup_{T\to\infty}\;\frac{\mathbb{E}\big[\sum_{t=L+1}^{T+L} C_t^{\pi_z}\big]}{\mathrm{OPT}(L,T)}=1.
\]
In particular, determine whether finite second moment \(\mathbb{E}[D^2]<\infty\) (or still weaker conditions) suffices in full generality, and if not, characterize a minimal condition that does.
latex:

Find the weakest (minimal) integrability/moment assumptions on the demand distribution \(D\) (beyond nonnegativity, non-determinism, and \(\mu=\mathbb{E}[D]<\infty\)) under which the constant-order policy \(\pi_z\) is asymptotically optimal as lead time grows, in the sense that
\[

location_in_paper: Page 6, Section 3.1 “Additional definitions and notations”, Remarks (bullet discussing moment assumptions).
open_problem_quote: By combining the arguments of the present paper (which assumes finite third moment but allows \(D\) to have bounded support) with the arguments of the aforementioned earlier version, we would in principle obtain asymptotic optimality assuming only a finite second moment. For simplicity of exposition, we do not formalize this generalization, and leave as an open question the minimal required assumptions on \(D\) for such an asymptotic optimality to hold.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for weakest distributional assumptions guaranteeing a universal asymptotic statement over all horizons and lead times, which is hard to refute by finite instance search. Automated search could suggest heavy-tailed distributions where performance degrades, but establishing failure would likely require asymptotic/probabilistic analysis rather than a finite counterexample.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can programmatically evaluate the constant-order policy and approximate OPT for small lead times/horizons via dynamic programming on discretized demand distributions to explore which tail behaviors appear problematic. However, the fitness signal is only a proxy for the true asymptotic regime and may be computationally expensive/noisy when approximating long lead times or near-critical \(r\uparrow\mathbb{E}[D]\).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
